sentences.text: Well-- [LAUGHTER]
It's hot.
There's things about Florida that I like and things that I don't.
The things that I like is I can wear shorts all the time-- [LAUGHS]
Comfortable clothes.
And-- am I on or what?
Florida is-- if I wouldn't be allergic to the sun and bug bites, then Florida is a excellent place.
You and my wife-- the bugs and the sun.
The bugs and the sun.
I have to be protected.
Because I'm allergic to the sun.
But it's a nice life in Florida.
It's--
I'll be nine years in September.
So I-- I enjoyed when my husband worked, because it was a normal life.
He went to work, and I did my thing.
Good morning, Lola.
Good morning, Chaim.
I was born in Lublin, Poland.
September the 23rd, 1925.
My mother and my father-- we were sort of religious-- not-- not real Hasidic--
--but Orthodox--
--house.
I was brought up.
I went to a Bais Yaakov.
And I had-- we were four sisters and three brothers.
I was the youngest.
Most of them were married when I, you know, grew up.
And they had children on their own.
They weren't living in the house.
My mother, and my father, and one sister.
She survived.
Her name is Gitel.
And she lives in the States on Staten Island.
As a young girl, it was difficult for me to go to a public school, because of my name.
Rabinowitz.
And the children, you know, the gentile children, they were very cruel.
They call you names, things like this.
In the beginning, I had gentile friends, because of the neighbors--
--very close friends.
And-- but then, when it starts getting worse, near the, you know--
Before 1939.
So we had to, even in Bais Yaakov, we had to go to school secretly, you know.
The school--
How you go secretly, because you should go to a public school.
Because it was mandatory to go to a school.
And that, according to the Polish or whatever-- that didn't count, you know, a religious school.
That was a religious school.
And then I-- I was in the-- enrolled in the-- my father was a Zionist.
It was always that [NON-ENGLISH].
It's got to be full, and you take it.
And on Friday nights, he had friends in with tea.
And they always discussed Israel.
His whole life was Israel.
A normal life--
--until they started-- there were programs here and there.
If any--
Yeah.
Before-- if anything happened, like a-- one Jewish boy and a Gentile boy-- he was, you know, a high in the government, a son of one of the--
--[NON-ENGLISH].
And he-- he was trying to be a macho guy, this Jewish guy and this gentile.
For a while, they would start to fight, you know, on the street.
And then one morning we got up, and he was killed-- that gentile guy.
The gentile boy was killed.
Was killed by the Jewish boy.
And then it was a black-- what was it-- Friday.
Every home was, you know, locked.
And there were pogroms and things.
So--
The-- the anti-Semites, the--
Ordinary people, you know, on a Sunday.
It was like-- like today, they have things.
It takes one.
You know, and they ganged up.
And they broke windows.
And on Friday night, you had to shut the-- the, you know, the drapes.
And you light your candles.
And that's how you live.
The outside that you light candles--
--on a Passover, the-- the-- that the Jews make matzah from gentile blood.
They need gentile blood.
The anti-Semitism--
--constantly.
So that-- that was our life-- constant fear.
You live in fear.
And then it was in 1939.
So we had to put up with what was going on in Germany. 1940-- [SIGHS]
--into Poland-- the German Jews--
--resettle them.
They throw us out from our homes.
They were concentrated in one-- in a room.
And they took everything.
And they gave to the Germans.
Forced out.
Yeah.
[SIGHS]
Well, you-- you only took-- [LAUGHS]
--whatever the necessity to-- Clothing?
--to-- to live.
Anything else-- you had good dishes or good-- good furniture, everything good, you know--
--was-- stayed there.
And it got so bad.
And the-- you know.
And you weren't allowed to do any-- anything to make any money, to survive on.
And food was scarce.
Even if you had the money, you couldn't get to it to buy anything.
Because the-- the Jews, they had stores.
They broke the windows.
They, you know, robbed them.
And it was very difficult.
I didn't understood, because then I had no idea what was going on.
But my father knew that it's so bad.
He felt probably sorry that he had a chance to go to Israel and-- you know.
He should have left.
But-- and his-- you know, you-- you have to leave family.
And--
Because he wore-- he had a beard, you know, a small like Theodor Herzl.
You know that picture?
Like, you know-- he-- he kept it nice, groomed and everything.
Yes.
Well, he had storage of all kind of grains-- wheat.
They used to bring the peasants--
--you know, from the farmers and the storage.
And then they used to ship all that wheat to-- I don't know, Germany, whatever, you know.
Yes.
We had food.
But then later, that everything was--
--confiscated, because of the-- it got so bad that my sister-- is the one she was married-- she lived at the outskirts of Lublin, called-- near Lublin-- Glusk.
It was like beautiful place.
And they had meals, you see?
All that meal-- flour-- the best flour, I remember-- they had all those meals.
And they were confiscated also.
And when the-- when the Germans moved in, they took the family.
The ones they were well off-- they picked on the first ones they were well off.
So my brother-in-law's-- brother-in-law and my father-- and the-- they took him out.
And they beat him up-- the Germans-- so bad that he spit up blood.
And then-- then that-- he recovered a little.
They took him back and forth, just because they had all those things that a Jew-- why should you have anything?
Right.
And they died.
They beat them to death.
They didn't--
That was my sister's brother-- my-- my brother-in-law's brother.
Brother-- and a whole bunch of other people.
You know, they were related.
They were in the mills, you know.
There was a big-- it was called Cross's Mill, the best flour in-- in probably in Poland.
Then it became so bad that I couldn't do anything, that I wanted to, like play with kids, or go out, or what.
We belonged-- I belonged to the chalutzim as a child.
And we didn't had any meetings.
My-- my parents wouldn't move, because it was too late.
Then, even if you wanna get-- we didn't had anybody in the United States to have a sponsor--
--to go to or what.
The only place was where you were born, and that-- our family.
So in 1942, they began to be that they gonna liquidate all the Jews, all the ghettos.
And they're gonna make clean from Jews, of course, judenrein.
I happened to be in the-- with the-- listening in where they-- they had a conference in the judenrein.
And I listened in.
They said, well, the girls have a chance to go to Germany on Aryan papers, you know, to hide as Aryans.
But for the boys, I'm afraid there is no chance.
It's a dead giveaway that they're Jewish.
Right.
They were killed.
The partisans did not care for the Jews being part of them, I remember.
They didn't-- they didn't want to accept them.
And one of them that I adored-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH].
He was, you know-- I-- I really was so attached to him.
And he went, and he was killed.
Yeah.
By the partisans.
And one went to Majdanek.
And he escaped from Majdanek last time.
I was told by my sister, because she kept in touch.
She was much older than I was.
So he escaped, and he was killed too.
I don't know.
They were killed.
'Cause the fight.
And all three were killed.
Whoever went, you know, into the forest to the partisans--
--they were killed.
And to the end, we went-- there is no place where to hide.
It was, you know, by Friday, they said they're gonna have to-- everybody's got to leave.
Not one Jew is gonna remain in the city.
And--
I was the only-- my-- my parents--
--they had a bunker--
--with a-- with, you know, friends of my father.
They figured the war was gonna only last maybe two more weeks.
So they're gonna survive.
They apparently had enough food, things what they needed.
Underground, a bunker.
Bunker.
Yeah.
And the-- [SIGHS] --they had everything in there.
And so we said goodbye.
My mother had her earrings-- she took them off.
She said to my sister-- I remember that was on a Friday-- you take them.
This is gonna probably help you to survive when you need, you know, to buy your time.
She said, I can't take them.
You keep them in your ears the way you always wore them.
And I was shivering.
I came up with the idea, actually, to go in on Aryan papers.
Because I hear-- overheard what they said in the, you know, in--
--about the Judenrein.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [CRYING] --she said, take care.
She should take care on me.
[CRYING]
She didn't leave.
I weren't in the bunker yet.
Because we said goodbye.
After we left.
We left.
We started walking.
[SIGHS] And we walked-- I don't know how long, maybe a 1/2 a day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No.
She kept the earrings.
She said, don't.
So we kept walking.
And then my sister remind herself, there is somebody who is doing something, you know, hiding you out.
She knew a woman.
This woman, she takes money for it.
And she hides you in-- because if it's only two weeks, you know, we can survive.
We walked in.
She said, OK.
And she took our things, what we had with us.
And she put them in-- there was-- she had one room in a barn where the cows--
--you know, next to the cows.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then she said, you can stay here.
That was on a Friday.
Saturday-- Sunday she went to church.
She said, she's going to church.
So my sister mentioned that my brother was supposed to, you know, hide out.
The mayor from the city-- Right.
--was friendly with him.
I don't know what-- maybe he did him favors before-- that he's gonna hide him out.
And she told her where to go.
She gave her our address.
She wrote down on a paper where to go and ask if he is still there.
She left, and she came back.
It was already like nighttime.
And she said, your brother is not there anymore.
And OK.
That was Sunday morning.
We stayed there.
Sunday-- no.
That was Sunday night already.
About-- we went to sleep.
It was some-- on the floor.
We slept on the floor with bugs and everything.
And about 12 o'clock, after 12:00, we heard a knock at the door.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she went.
She opened the door.
And three men walked in in, you know, German uniforms.
And the minute they walked in, we were frightened.
We didn't know.
And then she-- they recognized us.
They recognized my sister.
And they didn't said anything.
They-- they just looked.
And my--
Yeah.
So my sister said, we can pay you to leave us alone.
We had money.
[SIGHS] The first thing was said-- she took our things.
We had nothing, you know, with us.
She took all her things, and she put on the-- in the barn.
She hide our things.
How can we get some things back?
And then he told her to go and bring the things back.
By the time he told her to bring things back, he told us, run.
Don't look back.
He opened the door.
As fast as you can-- don't ever look back.
So did they take money from you, or they just let you go?
She gave them--
She gave them money.
But never mind the money.
I thought out of the goodness of their heart--
Yeah.
--they let you go.
So they opened the door.
Don't ever look back.
Just keep going-- running-- not going, but run.
And we walked out.
Like this--
First miracle-- that's true.
So we walked.
It was all night.
We didn't know which way we walked.
Apparently, it was-- it led us to a big forest.
They call them Black Forest.
We walked in this-- that forest.
We breathed a little easier, because there were no people.
We're gonna be, you know, only us and that-- those-- that forest.
But we walked into deep.
It was so dark that we haven't seen any sky.
And then we heard-- we walked a couple of hours.
We heard crying-- a baby crying so loud and woman's-- a woman's voice.
And we followed that, you know, sound from where it came.
And my sister walked in, and my sister recognized.
She knew the woman.
She had about a nine-month-old baby in her arms.
So she dried up.
She hadn't fed the baby for a couple of days maybe.
She said she was tearing her hair out.
And she was screaming.
My sister said to her, you want to join us?
She said, to leave the baby?
No, I want to die with the baby.
I can never do that-- leave the baby.
And we couldn't, you know, stay there, because she was screaming.
We were afraid that they-- somebody is gonna hear.
And we started to walk.
We walked-- it was already noon.
And there was no end to this forest.
Finally, we saw a little light.
And then we saw a path going.
And we walked through that path to lead us someplace.
When we walked out, it was a field of, you know, officers training on horses.
So my sister was panic.
She thought they-- they come and-- to-- to-- to, you know.
That they saw me-- I don't even-- I don't know if they saw us.
But she panicked.
And she said, you wanted to leave.
Because I was afraid, you know.
So she said, you wanted to leave.
She just throw me down.
And she said, you wanted to leave.
And she grabbed me, you know.
And--
Angry at me that I wanted to leave-- why she didn't went with the parents or-- or--
--or went to, you know, the-- on that to Kowel, to the concentration camp back, you know, like everybody else did.
And then, they-- eventually they turned around-- those-- those officers.
They had enough training.
They just turned around, and they went their way.
Then--
The didn't saw me.
That was about quite a distance.
But you saw them.
It was quite--
--a distance.
And even if they saw, they probably wouldn't know who it is--
--yet.
But we always had in that mind, you know, that everybody who is gonna see you--
--will kill-- will kill you because you're Jewish.
So then it was easier on-- on her.
I said, OK.
I brushed off.
And we started again.
We started walking.
And there was no end to that walk.
I was just field.
And it was already going to get dark.
And we didn't know where-- we are going to wind up in another forest?
Finally, we turned-- we turned on another, you know, path we saw.
And we started to this, but where it's gonna lead us?
So we saw smoke coming out of, you know, smoke.
Then it must be a house someplace.
If there's smoke, there's a house.
And we walked towards this house-- nearer.
[SIGHS] We walked.
So we walked in.
We didn't care what's gonna be anymore.
[SIGHS]
So we walked in, and he's-- he probably knew.
This man knew something.
Right.
And he said, we can stay in the barn.
Then he said to her, I have a bunker where they keep potatoes for the winter, and what.
And he's gonna keep her there.
Because he doesn't have any children.
[LAUGHS]
With my-- my sister.
In the meantime, he said, you can stay on-- on the-- in the-- on that-- in the barn--
--where the cows.
And you walked up on a ladder.
And there was straw there.
And we walked up.
We rested.
And then my sister said to me-- she didn't want to tell me what he told her.
And that-- [SIGHS]
--maybe I wouldn't understand then.
Right.
OK?
